definition: Combining multiple data points into a single summary value such as a sum, mean, or count.
Identifying data points that deviate significantly from expected patterns or distributions.
An ordered collection of elements of the same type, foundational to numerical computing.
The sum of all values in a dataset divided by the count of those values.
An algorithm computing gradients through a neural network to update weights during training.
A simple reference model used to measure whether a more complex model adds real value.
A subset of training data processed together in one forward and backward pass of a model.
Updating the probability of a hypothesis as new evidence is incorporated using Bayes' theorem.
Systematic error in a model that causes it to consistently miss the true value in one direction.
A prediction task with exactly two possible output categories, such as spam or not spam.
A variable representing discrete groups or labels rather than continuous numeric values.
The geometric center of a cluster, used as a reference point in algorithms like k-means.
A dataset condition where one category has significantly more samples than others.
A supervised learning task that predicts which category a new observation belongs to.
An unsupervised method grouping similar data points together without predefined labels.
A table comparing predicted vs actual class labels to evaluate a classification model.
A numeric variable that can take any value within a range, such as height or temperature.
A statistical measure of how strongly two variables move together, ranging from -1 to 1.
A technique splitting data into multiple train-test folds to produce a more reliable evaluation.
Comma-Separated Values — a plain text format for storing tabular data, one row per line.
The process of detecting and correcting errors, inconsistencies, and missing values in a dataset.
The gradual change in input data distribution over time that degrades model performance.
A centralized repository storing raw, unstructured data at scale for future processing.
An automated sequence of steps that ingests, transforms, and loads data from source to destination.
A structured storage system optimized for querying and analyzing large volumes of historical data.
A two-dimensional, labeled data structure with columns of potentially different types, as in pandas.
A flowchart-like model that splits data based on feature conditions to make predictions.
Reducing the number of features in a dataset while preserving as much information as possible.
A function describing the frequency of values across all possible outcomes in a dataset.
Exploratory Data Analysis — the initial investigation of data using statistics and visualizations.
One complete pass of the entire training dataset through a machine learning model.
Extract, Transform, Load — the process of moving and reshaping data between systems.
A quantitative measure used to assess how well a model performs on a given task.
The harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both in a single performance metric.
An individual measurable variable used as input to a machine learning model.
Creating or transforming input variables to improve a model's predictive power.
A score indicating how much each input variable contributed to a model's predictions.
An optimization algorithm iteratively adjusting model parameters to minimize a loss function.
The verified correct labels or values used to train and evaluate a machine learning model.
A portion of data withheld from training and used only for final model evaluation.
A configuration value set before training that controls how a model learns, not what it learns.
A statistical method determining whether observed data provides evidence against a null assumption.
Filling in missing values in a dataset using statistical estimates like the mean or median.
The phase where a trained model generates predictions on new, unseen input data.
The difference between the 75th and 25th percentile, measuring statistical spread robustly.
The target output value a supervised learning model is trained to predict.
A hyperparameter controlling how large each step is during gradient descent optimization.
A function measuring the difference between predicted and actual values during model training.
The middle value in an ordered dataset, robust to outliers unlike the arithmetic mean.
An absent data point in a dataset, represented as null or NaN, requiring handling before modeling.
A mathematical function learned from data that maps inputs to predicted outputs.
A condition where two or more input features are highly correlated, distorting model coefficients.
A symmetric, bell-shaped probability distribution where most values cluster around the mean.
Rescaling feature values to a standard range — typically 0 to 1 — before model training.
The default assumption of no effect or no difference that a statistical test attempts to reject.
A data point that differs markedly from other observations and may indicate noise or a real anomaly.
When a model learns the training data too precisely, performing poorly on new unseen data.
The probability of observing results at least as extreme as those seen, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
A Python library providing DataFrame structures for data manipulation and analysis.
The value below which a given percentage of observations in a dataset fall.
A chained sequence of preprocessing and modeling steps that can be fit and applied as a unit.
The proportion of positive predictions that were actually correct out of all positive predictions made.
The output a trained model generates when given a new input it has not seen before.
All data transformation steps applied before feeding data into a machine learning model.
An ensemble model combining many decision trees to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting.
The proportion of actual positives that a model correctly identified out of all real positives.
A supervised learning task predicting a continuous numeric output from input features.
A technique penalizing model complexity during training to reduce overfitting.
A subset of observations drawn from a larger population for analysis or model training.
A systematic error occurring when the collected data does not represent the full population.
A measure of the asymmetry of a distribution — positive skew has a longer right tail.
Dividing a dataset into separate portions for training a model and evaluating its performance.
A measure of how spread out values are relative to the mean in a dataset.
Training a model on labeled data where the correct output is known for each input.
The output column a model is trained to predict, also called the dependent variable.
The labeled dataset used to fit a machine learning model's parameters.
When a model is too simple to capture patterns in the data, performing poorly on both train and test sets.
Training a model on unlabeled data to discover hidden structure or patterns without guidance.
A model's sensitivity to fluctuations in training data — high variance models tend to overfit.
A one-dimensional array of numbers representing a data point or feature set in a model.
Representing data graphically to reveal patterns, trends, and outliers at a glance.
A learned parameter in a model that scales the importance of an input feature.
